content:
This feature can be used for publishing content such as:
 Project or software documentation Online courses Tutorials  The parent folder may be renamed, for example, to docs for project documentation or course for creating an online course.
To disable this feature, either delete the parent folder, or set draft = true in the front matter of all its pages.
After renaming or deleting the parent folder, you may wish to update any [[menu.main]] menu links to it in the config.toml.

content:
Click on the Slides button above to view the built-in slides feature.
 Slides can be added in a few ways:
 Create slides using Academic’s Slides feature and link using url_slides parameter in the front matter of the talk file Upload an existing slide deck to static/ and link using url_slides parameter in the front matter of the talk file Embed your slides (e.g. Google Slides) or presentation video on this page using shortcodes.  Further talk details can easily be added to this page using Markdown and $\rm \LaTeX$ math code.

title: Internal Project

content:
Academic makes it easy to create a beautiful website for free using Markdown. Customize anything on your site with widgets, themes, and language packs.
Follow our easy step by step guide to learn how to build your own free website with Academic. Check out the personal demo or the business demo of what you’ll get in less than 10 minutes.
 View the documentation Ask a question Request a feature or report a bug Updating? View the Update Guide and Release Notes Support development of Academic:  Donate a coffee Become a backer on Patreon Decorate your laptop or journal with an Academic sticker Wear the T-shirt   
Key features:
 Easily manage various content including homepage, blog posts, publications, talks, and projects Extensible via color themes and widgets/plugins Write in Markdown for easy formatting and code highlighting, with LaTeX for mathematical expressions Social/academic network linking, Google Analytics, and Disqus comments Responsive and mobile friendly Simple and refreshing one page design Multilingual and easy to customize  Color Themes Academic is available in different color themes and font themes.
         Ecosystem  Academic Admin: An admin tool to import publications from BibTeX or import assets for an offline site Academic Scripts: Scripts to help migrate content to new versions of Academic  Install You can choose from one of the following four methods to install:
 one-click install using your web browser (recommended) install on your computer using Git with the Command Prompt/Terminal app install on your computer by downloading the ZIP files install on your computer with RStudio  Quick install using your web browser  Install Academic with Netlify  Netlify will provide you with a customizable URL to access your new site  On GitHub, go to your newly created academic-kickstart repository and edit config.toml to personalize your site. Shortly after saving the file, your site will automatically update Read the Quick Start Guide to learn how to add Markdown content. For inspiration, refer to the Markdown content which powers the Demo  Install with Git Prerequisites:
 Download and install Git Download and install Hugo   Fork the Academic Kickstart repository and clone your fork with Git:
git clone https://github.com/sourcethemes/academic-kickstart.git My_Website  Note that if you forked Academic Kickstart, the above command should be edited to clone your fork, i.e. replace sourcethemes with your GitHub username.
 Initialize the theme:
cd My_Website git submodule update --init --recursive   Install with ZIP  Download and extract Academic Kickstart Download and extract the Academic theme to the themes/academic/ folder from the above step  Install with RStudio View the guide to installing Academic with RStudio
Quick start  If you installed on your computer, view your new website by running the following command:
hugo server  Now visit localhost:1313 and your new Academic powered website will appear. Otherwise, if using Netlify, they will provide you with your URL.
 Read the Quick Start Guide to learn how to add Markdown content, customize your site, and deploy it. For inspiration, refer to the Markdown content which powers the Demo
 Build your site by running the hugo command. Then host it for free using Github Pages or Netlify (refer to the first installation method). Alternatively, copy the generated public/ directory (by FTP, Rsync, etc.) to your production web server (such as a university’s hosting service).
  Updating Feel free to star the project on Github to help keep track of updates and check out the release notes prior to updating your site.
Before updating the framework, it is recommended to make a backup of your entire website directory (or at least your themes/academic directory) and record your current version number.
By default, Academic is installed as a Git submodule which can be updated by running the following command:
git submodule update --remote --merge  Check out the update guide for full instructions and alternative methods.
Feedback & Contributing Please use the issue tracker to let me know about any bugs or feature requests, or alternatively make a pull request.
For support, head over to the Hugo discussion forum.
License Copyright 2016-present George Cushen.
Released under the MIT license.
summary: Create a beautifully simple website or blog in under 10 minutes.
title: Academic: the website designer for Hugo

content: More detail can easily be written here using Markdown and $\rm \LaTeX$ math code.
summary: Person re-identification is a critical security task for recognizing a person across spatially disjoint sensors. Previous work can be computationally intensive and is mainly based on low-level cues extracted from RGB data and implemented on a PC for a fixed sensor network (such as traditional CCTV). We present a practical and efficient framework for mobile devices (such as smart phones and robots) where high-level semantic soft biometrics are extracted from RGB and depth data. By combining these cues, our approach attempts to provide robustness to noise, illumination, and minor variations in clothing. This mobile approach may be particularly useful for the identification of persons in areas ill-served by fixed sensors or for tasks where the sensor position and direction need to dynamically adapt to a target. Results on the BIWI dataset are preliminary but encouraging. Further evaluation and demonstration of the system will be available on our website.
title: A Person Re-Identification System For Mobile Devices

content:
R MarkdownThis is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com.
You can embed an R code chunk like this:
summary(cars)## speed dist ## Min. : 4.0 Min. : 2.00 ## 1st Qu.:12.0 1st Qu.: 26.00 ## Median :15.0 Median : 36.00 ## Mean :15.4 Mean : 42.98 ## 3rd Qu.:19.0 3rd Qu.: 56.00 ## Max. :25.0 Max. :120.00fit <- lm(dist ~ speed, data = cars)fit## ## Call:## lm(formula = dist ~ speed, data = cars)## ## Coefficients:## (Intercept) speed ## -17.579 3.932Including PlotsYou can also embed plots. See Figure 1 for example:
par(mar = c(0, 1, 0, 1))pie(c(280, 60, 20),c('Sky', 'Sunny side of pyramid', 'Shady side of pyramid'),col = c('#0292D8', '#F7EA39', '#C4B632'),init.angle = -50, border = NA)Figure 1: A fancy pie chart.

content: More detail can easily be written here using Markdown and $\rm \LaTeX$ math code.
summary: We present a mobile visual clothing search system whereby a smart phone user can either choose a social networking photo or take a new photo of a person wearing clothing of interest and search for similar clothing in a retail database. From the query image, the person is detected, clothing is segmented, and clothing features are extracted and quantized. The information is sent from the phone client to a server, where the feature vector of the query image is used to retrieve similar clothing products from online databases. The phone's GPS location is used to re-rank results by retail store location. State of the art work focuses primarily on the recognition of a diverse range of clothing offline and pays little attention to practical applications. Evaluated on a challenging dataset, the system is relatively fast and achieves promising results.
title: Mobile visual clothing search

content:
IntroductionIn this example we explore “generalized random forests” (grf) to estimate treatment effects in the setting of a randomized experiment.
Generalized Random Forests (grf) is a package based on a paper by Susan Athey, Julie Tibshirani, Stefan Wager. grf provides methods to estimate treatment effects.
The algorithm is comparable to “normal” random forests, in the sense that it estimates an outcome based on the average estimation in many regression trees. There are a few differences with a normal random forest:
In a normal random forest the splitting in the regression trees is based on minimizing the “Root Mean Square Error” (RSME), while in grf splitting is based on maximum heterogeneity of the treatment effect.In addition to a normal random forest, grf uses a subsample of the data in to construct the trees, while the other part of the data is used to estimate the treatment effect based on the constructed trees.Source
The attractive part of grf is that it:
provides estimates of the individual treatment effectwith confidence intervalsestimates average treatment effects (ate)estimates conditional treatment effects (cate)In this notebook we will run a grf on simulated data and test how well grf performs.
Please note that we have assumed in the data that treatment assignment was random.
Packageslibrary(tidyverse)library(ranger)library(caret)library(grf)library(cowplot)library(PerformanceAnalytics)DataFirst we construct a list with 10000 persons and make a data frame of it.
ID <- 1:10000df <- as.data.frame(ID)We will add the following variables:
agehaving a chronic disease (0,1)SES (1,2,3)Treatment (0,1)NoiseTreatment_effect (as a function and age)HC as a function of age, chronic, SES and treatmentset.seed(123)df <- df %>%mutate(age= round(abs(rnorm(10000,45, 50)),0)) %>%mutate(Chronic = rbinom(10000, 1, 0.05)) %>%mutate(SES = sample(c(1,2,3), size = 10000, replace = T)) %>%mutate(Treatment = sample(c(0,1), size= 10000, replace = T)) %>%mutate(Noise = rnorm(10000, 0, 1000)) %>%mutate(Treatment_effect = -600 - age^2 - age * SES*100 - Chronic*1000 ) %>%mutate(HC = 10000 + age^2 + 3000* Chronic + 4 * SES + Treatment_effect * Treatment + Noise )summary(df)## ID age Chronic SES ## Min. : 1 Min. : 0.00 Min. :0.000 Min. :1.000 ## 1st Qu.: 2501 1st Qu.: 24.00 1st Qu.:0.000 1st Qu.:1.000 ## Median : 5000 Median : 48.00 Median :0.000 Median :2.000 ## Mean : 5000 Mean : 54.98 Mean :0.049 Mean :1.977 ## 3rd Qu.: 7500 3rd Qu.: 80.00 3rd Qu.:0.000 3rd Qu.:3.000 ## Max. :10000 Max. :237.00 Max. :1.000 Max. :3.000 ## Treatment Noise Treatment_effect HC ## Min. :0.0000 Min. :-3497.287 Min. :-123261 Min. :-56008 ## 1st Qu.:0.0000 1st Qu.: -668.315 1st Qu.: -22621 1st Qu.: 1131 ## Median :0.0000 Median : -6.979 Median : -11956 Median : 9116 ## Mean :0.4997 Mean : 0.643 Mean : -16008 Mean : 6644 ## 3rd Qu.:1.0000 3rd Qu.: 686.919 3rd Qu.: -5325 3rd Qu.: 12764 ## Max. :1.0000 Max. : 3982.778 Max. : -600 Max. : 63060We do not want negative ages, therefore we made age an absolute value. We did not change very old people (e.g. age>120), because we want some extreme values in our dataset. We constructed a noise variable, but will not use it (in this stage).
The analist only has the following dataset
df_analist <- df %>%select(ID, age, Chronic, SES, Treatment, HC)head(df_analist)## ID age Chronic SES Treatment HC## 1 1 17 1 1 0 12497.792## 2 2 33 0 3 1 -1623.111## 3 3 123 0 1 0 25713.101## 4 4 49 0 2 0 12927.043## 5 5 51 0 1 0 12812.685## 6 6 131 0 2 0 26685.593suppressWarnings(chart.Correlation(df_analist))AS we can see in our data health care cost is very skewed with a long tail.
Analysis with generalized random forestsPlan of actionWe will split the data in a train and test setWe will run a grf analysis to estimate the individual treatment effectWe will compare the estimated individual treatment effect versus the real treatment effectWe will try to identify the most important variables that drive the estimation of the individual treatment effect by:the variable importance by the grf packagea linear regression of the predictors on the estimated treatment effecta normal random forest of the estimated treatment effect on the predictorsWe will compare the estimated average treatment effect with the real average treatment effectSplit the dataTrain Test
set.seed(123)trainIndex <- createDataPartition(df$HC, p = .5,list = FALSE,times = 1)dfTrain <- df[ trainIndex,]dfTest <- df[-trainIndex,]We will then construct a dataframe with the co_variates \(X\).
X_train <- dfTrain %>%select(age, Chronic, SES)X_test1 <- dfTest %>%select(age, Chronic, SES)Change train en test data frames in matrices
\(X\) co-variates \(Y\) Outcome \(W\) Treatment
X <- as.matrix(X_train)Y <- as.matrix(dfTrain$HC)W <- as.matrix(dfTrain$Treatment)X_test <- as.matrix(X_test1)Estimate heterogenous treatment effectsRun a causal forest (without tuning)
set.seed(123)cf <- causal_forest(X,Y,W, num.trees = 5000)pred <- predict(cf, X_test, estimate.variance = TRUE)Add predictions and confidence intervals to the test set
dfTest <- dfTest %>%mutate(Predictions_cf = as.vector(pred$predictions)) %>%mutate(Variance = as.vector(pred$variance.estimates))%>%mutate(Lci = Predictions_cf - 1.96* sqrt(Variance)) %>%mutate(Uci = Predictions_cf + 1.96* sqrt(Variance))Compare estimated individual treatment effects to the real individual treatment effectsggplot(data=dfTest, aes(x=Treatment_effect, y=Predictions_cf)) + geom_point(color="blue") + theme_classic() +geom_linerange(aes(ymin=Lci, ymax=Uci))+ geom_abline(slope = 1 , intercept = 0, col="green") + xlim(-120000,0) + ylim(-120000,0)Causal forest seems to predict fairly well
The correlation between the prediction and the actual treatment effect is 99%.
df_CC <- dfTest %>%select(Predictions_cf, Treatment_effect)suppressWarnings(chart.Correlation(df_CC))The prediction seems to go wrong at extreme values, but for the majority of the observations, the prediction loss (the actual treatment effect minus the predicted treatment effect) is fairly close to zero. There are a few predictions extremely wrong.
dfTest <- dfTest %>%mutate(Prediction_loss = Treatment_effect - Predictions_cf)ggplot(data= dfTest, aes(Prediction_loss)) + geom_histogram(aes(y=..density..), bins=1000, fill= "blue", alpha= .7) +geom_density(alpha=.5, fill="red") Heterogenous treatment effectWe know that the “real” treatment effects are hetergenous (dependent on age and a little bit on SES and Age). We can see if the predictions are right wrt to this effect:
Variable Importance from the package grfVarImpplot <- data.frame(Name = colnames(cf$X.orig), Value = variable_importance(cf))ggplot(VarImpplot, aes(x= reorder(Name, Value), y=Value)) + geom_point(col = "blue", size =3)+ coord_flip() + geom_linerange(aes(ymin=0, ymax= Value)) + ylim(0,1) + ylab("variable importance")+ xlab("")Linear regressionreg1 <- lm(Predictions_cf ~ age + Chronic + SES, data= dfTest)summary(reg1)## ## Call:## lm(formula = Predictions_cf ~ age + Chronic + SES, data = dfTest)## ## Residuals:## Min 1Q Median 3Q Max ## -11483.8 -1483.9 796.2 1957.2 17282.4 ## ## Coefficients:## Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|) ## (Intercept) 8944.677 117.845 75.902 <2e-16 ***## age -295.798 1.011 -292.541 <2e-16 ***## Chronic 59.220 177.123 0.334 0.738 ## SES -4357.655 48.318 -90.187 <2e-16 ***## ---## Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1## ## Residual standard error: 2764 on 4996 degrees of freedom## Multiple R-squared: 0.9491, Adjusted R-squared: 0.9491 ## F-statistic: 3.108e+04 on 3 and 4996 DF, p-value: < 2.2e-16Random Forestset.seed(123)rf_trainIndex <- createDataPartition(dfTest$ID, p = .8,list = FALSE,times = 1)rfTrain <- dfTest[ rf_trainIndex,]rfTest <- dfTest[-rf_trainIndex,]set.seed(123)rfcontrol <- trainControl( method = "cv",number = 10)rf <- train(Predictions_cf ~ age + Chronic + SES, data = rfTrain, method = "ranger",importance = "permutation",trControl = rfcontrol)## note: only 2 unique complexity parameters in default grid. Truncating the grid to 2 .plot(varImp(rf))Average Treatment Effectgrf presents the possibility to calculate the average treatment effect,
There are different options to calculate the average treatment effect. target.sample= “overlap” is preferred over target.sample = “all” when
In this case we have randomized treatment, therefore I think it is ok to choose option “all”
average_treatment_effect(cf, target.sample = "overlap")## estimate std.err ## -15956.8012 206.2091average_treatment_effect(cf, target.sample = "all")## estimate std.err ## -15999.52057 52.71449mean(df$Treatment_effect)## [1] -16008.12The estimated average treatment effect is fairly close to the “real” average treatment-effect.
Heterogenous treatment effectWe know that the “real” treatment effects are hetergenous (i.e. a linear function of age with formule -600 + 20* age). We can see if the predictions are right wrt to this effect:
plot(variable_importance(cf)) We could also make plot:
df_pa <- dfTest %>%select(Predictions_cf, age, Chronic, SES)suppressWarnings(chart.Correlation(df_pa))With this plot, we are able to say something about the heterogenous treatment effects.
summary:
IntroductionIn this example we explore “generalized random forests” (grf) to estimate treatment effects in the setting of a randomized experiment.
Generalized Random Forests (grf) is a package based on a paper by Susan Athey, Julie Tibshirani, Stefan Wager. grf provides methods to estimate treatment effects.
The algorithm is comparable to “normal” random forests, in the sense that it estimates an outcome based on the average estimation in many regression trees.
title: Causal inference example

content:
Welcome to Slides Academic
Features  Efficiently write slides in Markdown 3-in-1: Create, Present, and Publish your slides Supports speaker notes Mobile friendly slides  Controls  Next: Right Arrow or Space Previous: Left Arrow Start: Home Finish: End Overview: Esc Speaker notes: S Fullscreen: F Zoom: Alt + Click PDF Export: E  Code Highlighting Inline code: variable
Code block:
porridge = "blueberry" if porridge == "blueberry": print("Eating...")  Math In-line math: $x + y = z$
Block math:
$$ f\left( x \right) = \;\frac{{2\left( {x + 4} \right)\left( {x - 4} \right)}}{{\left( {x + 4} \right)\left( {x + 1} \right)}} $$
Fragments Make content appear incrementally
{{% fragment %}} One {{% /fragment %}} {{% fragment %}} **Two** {{% /fragment %}} {{% fragment %}} Three {{% /fragment %}}  Press Space to play!
One  Two  Three 
A fragment can accept two optional parameters:
 class: use a custom style (requires definition in custom CSS) weight: sets the order in which a fragment appears  Speaker Notes Add speaker notes to your presentation
{{% speaker_note %}} - Only the speaker can read these notes - Press `S` key to view {{% /speaker_note %}}  Press the S key to view the speaker notes!
 Only the speaker can read these notes Press S key to view   Themes  black: Black background, white text, blue links (default) white: White background, black text, blue links league: Gray background, white text, blue links beige: Beige background, dark text, brown links sky: Blue background, thin dark text, blue links   night: Black background, thick white text, orange links serif: Cappuccino background, gray text, brown links simple: White background, black text, blue links solarized: Cream-colored background, dark green text, blue links  Custom Slide Customize the slide style and background
{{< slide background-image="/img/boards.jpg" >}} {{< slide background-color="#0000FF" >}} {{< slide class="my-style" >}}  Custom CSS Example Let’s make headers navy colored.
Create assets/css/reveal_custom.css with:
.reveal section h1, .reveal section h2, .reveal section h3 { color: navy; }  Questions? Ask
Documentation
summary:

Features  Efficiently write slides in Markdown 3-in-1: Create, Present, and Publish your slides Supports speaker notes Mobile friendly slides  Controls  Next: Right Arrow or Space Previous: Left Arrow Start: Home Finish: End Overview: Esc Speaker notes: S Fullscreen: F Zoom: Alt + Click PDF Export: E  Code Highlighting Inline code: variable
Code block:
porridge = "blueberry" if porridge == "blueberry": print("Eating...")  Math In-line math: $x + y = z$
